<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><p>You're afraid of rejection.</p><p>Let’s analyze the stats for a minute.</p><p>Let’s say you get 20 no’s or better yet 100 or 1000 no's... before you get a yes. Would it be worth it?</p><p>But wait. Wouldn't it depend on the question that you're asking though?</p><p>What if the question you asked a complete stranger was: "Will you leave with me right now on a road trip from New York to California?"</p><p>And fourteen people rejected you... but the fifteenth person said yes.</p><p>Would you dwell on those fourteen people or would you start packing? And go rent a car?</p><p>Funny thing is... Those aren't even the correct odds. They're actually much better. But you would never know that because you haven’t tried. You would never know that because all you do is philosophize and refuse to put your thoughts into action.</p><p>You use other people's stats. Instead of getting your own statistic through your own experience.</p><p>If you put yourself on the line for a few days you’d see that rejection doesn’t happen as much as you think it does.</p><p>You know why?</p><p>Because EVERYONE wants to connect.</p><p>And if the quality of your crazy idea is good enough, only an idiot or a coward would reject it.</p><p>So if you're afraid hearing the word 'no'... just ask a better question.</p><p></p>
</div>
